articles.title: Statistical Detection of Bias in Professional Sports Refereeing
articles.subtitle: Theoretical foundations, Python implementation and empirical results of a referee decision analysis system
articles.note: Original paper written in Portuguese. This is the English translation.
articles.excerpt: Sports refereeing is a domain widely recognised as susceptible to cognitive and social biases, with direct impact on sporting outcomes and the economic values involved in professional football. This paper describes the theoretical foundations, computational implementation and empirical results of the CAL system, developed for the Portuguese Primeira Liga.

content.text: Pedro M. Pestana · CAL — Criticar a Arbitragem Legalmente · Lisbon, Portugal · 2026
Abstract

Sports refereeing is a domain widely recognised as susceptible to cognitive and social biases, with direct impact on sporting outcomes and the economic values involved in professional football. This paper describes the theoretical foundations, computational implementation and empirical results of the CAL system (Criticar a Arbitragem Legalmente — Legally Critiquing Refereeing), developed for the Portuguese Primeira Liga. For each statistical concept, both the mathematical formulation and the corresponding Python implementation are presented. The system covers 2,673 matches across 9 seasons (2017/18–2025/26). Following three rounds of independent critical review, the following corrections were made: (1) the binomial Z-score variance formula [ΣPᵢ(1-Pᵢ) instead of exp·(1-exp/n)]; (2) multiple testing correction now applies Benjamini-Hochberg across all 510 p-values as a single global family. The formal overdispersion test (Cameron & Trivedi, 1990) confirms that variance is only 1.39% above the Poisson expectation — negligible in magnitude despite being statistically significant with n=4,816. After global FDR at 5%, 11 robust statistical deviation patterns were identified in fouls across 8 distinct referees; no yellow card pattern survived correction. The system operates at the level of a solid exploratory analytical tool, not a strong causal inference instrument.
Keywords: sports refereeing; binomial Z-score; Poisson; Benjamini-Hochberg; FDR; overdispersion; Python; PostgreSQL; Liga Portugal.
1. Introduction

Professional football is one of the most economically and culturally significant phenomena of the 21st century. The Portuguese Primeira Liga moves hundreds of millions of euros per season in television rights, transfers and matchday revenues. Refereeing assumes a dimension that transcends the pitch: a single refereeing decision can determine access to European competitions, with a direct financial impact of several million euros.

The scientific literature on referee bias demonstrates that referees are susceptible to external influences — crowd pressure (Nevill, Balmer & Williams, 2002; Garicano, Palacios-Huerta & Prendergast, 2005), team status (Sutter & Kocher, 2004) and decision fatigue (Dohmen, 2008). Most of these studies analyse large leagues; the Portuguese reality lacks systematic analysis.

The CAL system aims to address this gap with a rigorous, transparent and reproducible quantitative approach, applying statistical models to 2,673 matches across 9 seasons. This paper documents three rounds of independent critical review and the resulting methodological corrections, following the principle that transparency about corrected errors is a form of scientific rigour, not a weakness.
2. Literature Review
2.1. Referee Bias: Empirical Evidence

Nevill, Balmer and Williams (2002) demonstrated that experienced referees make different decisions when exposed to crowd noise. In 40 referees who assessed the same 47 incidents with and without crowd sound, the presence of noise significantly reduced the number of fouls awarded against the home team.

Garicano, Palacios-Huerta and Prendergast (2005) demonstrated that referees award more injury time when the home team is losing by a narrow margin. Sutter and Kocher (2004) estimated that approximately 30% of the home advantage in the Bundesliga is attributable to biased refereeing decisions. Dohmen (2008) showed that bias manifests primarily in subjective decisions (penalties, cards), not in objective facts (offside).

Scoppa (2021) analysed the Italian Serie A during the crowd-free period (COVID-19), confirming that the elimination of spectators significantly reduced bias in favour of the home team — robust causal evidence of the social pressure mechanism.
2.2. Sports Analytics

The analytics revolution initiated with Moneyball (Lewis, 2003) reached football through metrics such as expected goals (xG). These metrics share with CAL the principle of comparing observed outcomes against those expected under a neutral model. For the Portuguese context, Leite (2013) found patterns consistent with referee bias, but without sufficient granularity for per-referee analysis.
2.3. Detection Methodologies and Gaps

Dixon and Robinson (1998) and Karlis and Ntzoufras (2003) proposed Poisson models for football, an approach that CAL adapts for expected fouls. Leave-one-season-out (LOSO) cross-validation is the recommended practice for temporal data (James et al., 2013). Multiple testing correction, frequently neglected in sports analytics, is mandatory when testing dozens of referees simultaneously (Benjamini & Hochberg, 1995). Gelman and Hill (2007) recommend mixed-effects models for hierarchical data such as that used in this analysis — a recognised limitation of the current system.
3. Statistical Models and Implementation
3.1. Feature Matrix

The feature matrix is constructed in cal/features/engineering.py with one row per team per match. The shift(1) on rolling averages prevents look-ahead bias in team strength and referee tendency variables.

# cal/features/engineering.py
df['team_strength'] = (df.groupby('team_id')['points']
    .transform(lambda x: x.shift(1).rolling(10, min_periods=3).mean()))
df['referee_yc_tendency'] = (df.groupby('referee_id')['yellow_cards']
    .transform(lambda x: x.shift(1).rolling(20, min_periods=5).mean()))
df['ht_score_diff'] = df['ht_goals_for'] - df['ht_goals_against']
Listing 1 — Feature matrix (cal/features/engineering.py)
3.2. Card Model (xYC and xRC)
P(Y=1|X) = σ(β₀ + β₁·is_home + β₂·matchday + β₃·team_strength + β₄·opp_strength + β₅·referee_tendency)

# cal/models/train.py — LOSO validation
for test_season in df['season'].unique():
    train = df[df['season'] != test_season]
    test  = df[df['season'] == test_season].copy()
    pipe = Pipeline([('scaler', StandardScaler()),
                     ('model', LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=500))])
    pipe.fit(train[FEATURES_YC], train['got_yellow'])
    test['expected_yellows'] = pipe.predict_proba(test[FEATURES_YC])[:, 1]
    # LOSO results: Brier Score xYC=0.067 (null: 0.078, 14% gain)
Listing 2 — xYC training with LOSO
Card Bias Z-score — Corrected Formula (v0.8.0)
Z_Y = (Σ Y_obs − Σ Pᵢ) / √(Σ Pᵢ · (1 − Pᵢ))

Correction v0.8.0: the previous formula used √(exp·(1-exp/n)), which is mathematically incorrect for non-i.i.d. Bernoullis. The correct variance is ΣPᵢ(1-Pᵢ) — the sum of individual per-match variances (Agresti, 2002, Ch. 1).

# cal/bias_engine.py — CORRECTED formula (v0.8.0)
# BEFORE (v0.7.x — incorrect): var_y = exp_y * (1 - exp_y / n)
# AFTER (correct):
p_i_y = grp['exp_yellows'].clip(0, 1)        # P(card) per match
var_y = float((p_i_y * (1 - p_i_y)).sum())   # Σ Pᵢ(1-Pᵢ)
z_y   = (obs_y - exp_y) / np.sqrt(var_y) if var_y > 0 else 0.0
Listing 3 — Binomial variance correction
3.3. Foul Model (xF)
log(E[F|X]) = β₀ + β₁·is_home + β₂·matchday + β₃·ht_score_diff + β₄·team_strength + β₅·opp_strength + β₆·referee_fouls_tendency
Z_F = (μ_obs − μ_exp) / (σ_obs / √n)

Note: for n < 30, the Normal approximation is imprecise. Ideally, thresholds from the t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom should be used. This limitation primarily affects referees with few seasons.
3.4. SuspicionScore
An ordinal metric of aggregate intensity with no known statistical distribution. It must not be used as a hypothesis test — it serves solely to rank referees.
SuspicionScore = |Z_yellows| + |Z_reds| + |Z_fouls|
3.5. Multiple Testing Correction — Global FDR (v0.9.1)

With 510 simultaneous tests (170 referee-season combinations × 3 metrics), the probability of false positives without correction is substantial. The Benjamini-Hochberg correction is applied to the TOTAL set of 510 p-values as a single global family:

# cal/bias_engine.py — GLOBAL FDR (v0.9.1, corrects previous error)
# BEFORE (v0.9.0 — incorrect): FDR applied separately per metric
# AFTER (correct): stack all p-values into a single family
all_pvals = pd.concat([
    result['p_value_yellow'].rename('p').to_frame().assign(metric='yellow'),
    result['p_value_red'].rename('p').to_frame().assign(metric='red'),
    result['p_value_fouls'].rename('p').to_frame().assign(metric='fouls'),
], ignore_index=True)
all_pvals['p_adj'] = fdr_correction(all_pvals['p']).values  # 510 tests
# Redistribute adjusted p-values back to original per-metric columns
Listing 4 — Global FDR across 510 p-values (cal/bias_engine.py)
3.6. Formal Overdispersion Test (Cameron & Trivedi, 1990)

# cal/bias_engine.py — formal Cameron & Trivedi (1990) test
# Dependent variable: (Y - mu)^2 - Y
# Regressor: mu^2 (no intercept)
y_aux = (obs - exp) ** 2 - obs
x_aux = exp ** 2
alpha = float(np.dot(x_aux, y_aux) / np.dot(x_aux, x_aux))
# Empirical result: alpha=0.0139, t=8.183, p≈0
# Interpretation: statistically significant overdispersion
#   but negligible magnitude (1.39% above Poisson)
# Critical distinction: statistical significance ≠ practical significance
Listing 5 — Formal overdispersion test
3.7. Alert Thresholds
Thresholds are indicative. Statistically robust evidence is defined exclusively by the FDR correction (p_adj < 0.05):

items: |Z| ≥ 1.0 — Monitor: pattern to follow up
|Z| ≥ 2.5 — Suspicious: pattern unlikely without correction
|Z| ≥ 3.0 — Anomaly: pattern very unlikely without correction
p_adj < 0.05 — FDR significant: robust evidence after global correction

content.text: A Z-score of 2.5 or 3.0 without FDR confirmation should be interpreted as a signal for more detailed analysis, not as evidence of bias.
4. Home Bias Analysis by Scoreline Context

CAL segments referee decisions into three half-time scoreline contexts: HOME_WINNING, DRAW and HOME_LOSING. The PressureBiasIndex measures whether the referee treats teams differently depending on context:
PressureBiasIndex = mean_fouls_diff(HOME_LOSING) − mean_fouls_diff(HOME_WINNING)

A positive value indicates that the referee favours the home team more when it is losing — a context of greater crowd pressure. This analysis partially controls for the omitted variable bias critique by segmenting by scoreline context, although the scoreline context is itself endogenous to the relative quality of the teams (a documented limitation).
5. Empirical Results
5.1. Overdispersion — Statistical vs. Practical Significance

The formal Cameron & Trivedi (1990) test returns alpha=0.0139, t=8.183, p≈0. There is statistically significant overdispersion, but the magnitude is negligible: the observed variance is only 1.39% above that expected under Poisson. With n=4,816, effects of this magnitude achieve high statistical significance without practical relevance. The Poisson model is adequate; the overdispersion constitutes a minor limitation to be documented.
5.2. Global FDR Results (510 tests)

items: Yellow cards: 0 significant patterns after global FDR (best p_adj = 0.548).
Fouls: 11 significant patterns across 8 distinct referees.
Red cards: 0 significant patterns.

content.text: Fundamental conclusion: there is no statistically robust evidence of yellow card deviations in the Primeira Liga during the period analysed. The 11 foul patterns remained identical whether FDR was applied per metric separately or globally — the lowest foul p-values (< 0.002) are sufficiently distant from those of yellow cards (minimum 0.003) that global application does not alter the conclusions.
5.3. Referees with Robust Foul Patterns
Listing 6 — Referees with robust evidence after global FDR (diff = observed - expected fouls per match)

The negative sign dominates (7 of 8 referees): a pattern of fewer fouls awarded than the model expects, consistent with a more permissive style. Cláudio Filipe Ruivo Pereira presents the most robust pattern: Z=-5.279 in 2022/23 (p_adj≈0, 28 matches) and Z=-3.447 in 2023/24 (p_adj=0.014, 36 matches).
5.4. Interpretation: Style vs. Bias

A negative foul deviation (fewer fouls than expected) may reflect two distinct mechanisms: (1) a systematically more permissive refereeing style, independent of the teams involved; or (2) bias in favour of specific teams that would commit more fouls without being penalised. Distinguishing these mechanisms requires cross-referencing foul Z-scores with yellow card Z-scores.

-- Correlation Z_fouls vs Z_yellows for the 8 referees with FDR significance
SELECT r.name, s.season,
       ROUND(s.fouls_diff_bias_z::numeric,3)  AS z_fouls,
       ROUND(s.yellow_diff_bias_z::numeric,3) AS z_yellows
FROM referee_bias_scores s JOIN referees r USING (referee_id)
WHERE s.sig_fouls_fdr = true
ORDER BY r.name, s.season;
Listing 7 — Query for style vs. bias analysis
This analysis should be carried out as a next step before any public interpretation of the identified patterns.
5.5. Statistical Power Analysis
The ability to detect an effect depends on sample size. For the foul t-test with alpha=0.05 and 80% power, the approximate minimum requirements are:

items: Large effect (|d|=0.8): n ≥ 14 matches
Medium effect (|d|=0.5): n ≥ 34 matches
Small effect (|d|=0.2): n ≥ 200 matches

content.text: With the minimum of 10 matches per season adopted by the system, only large effects are detectable with 80% power. The absence of a signal for referees with few seasons cannot be interpreted as the absence of a pattern.
6. System Architecture
CAL runs on Docker with 20 PostgreSQL tables and a Streamlit dashboard with seven analytical views. The execution pipeline is idempotent:

python run_ingest.py   '2024/25'   # ingest from football-data.co.uk
python run_referees.py '2024/25'   # referees via Sofascore
python run_features.py '2024/25'   # feature engineering
python run_models.py               # LOSO training
python run_bias.py                 # Z-scores + global FDR + overdispersion
python run_cards.py                # fouls/yellow per team
python run_bias_history.py         # per-matchday history
python run_alerts.py   '2024/25'   # automated alerts
python run_home_bias.py            # home bias by scoreline context
Listing 8 — Full pipeline
7. Limitations and Ethical Considerations
7.1. Methodological Limitations

First, the model does not control for unobserved team heterogeneity — playing styles, tactical aggression, competitive context. A high Z-score may result from specific matchups rather than referee bias. This is the most significant limitation and cannot be resolved with the data currently available.

Second, observations are not strictly independent: consecutive matches have temporal correlation. Mixed-effects models with random effects per referee and team (Gelman & Hill, 2007) would be more rigorous but require a substantial rewrite of the model layer.

Third, the ht_score_diff variable has a partial specification problem: the half-time score may itself be a consequence of first-half refereeing decisions. A complete solution would require minute-level event data.

Fourth, alert thresholds are based on the Normal distribution. For n < 30, the foul statistic follows a t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom, and the quantiles differ from those of the Normal — for n=5, the 97.5th percentile is 2.78 (vs. 1.96 for the Normal). Using Normal thresholds for small samples is conservatively imprecise.

Fifth, the model assumes that the league-average referee represents the neutral reference point. If the league average is itself systematically biased, the system detects deviations relative to an already distorted baseline.

Sixth, the absence of VAR data prevents the construction of confirmed error metrics (IELC) and win probability change metrics (ΔWP). These metrics would have greater content validity but require sources not freely available.
7.2. Epistemological Status

The CAL system is a solid exploratory analytical tool. It identifies statistical deviations consistent with bias — it does not prove causal bias. The absence of strong causal inference results from data and model specification limitations, not from flaws in the overall design. Transparency about these limitations is a form of scientific rigour.
7.3. Ethical Considerations

CAL was designed as a public scrutiny tool, not an accusatory instrument. The terminology used (suspicious, anomaly) is technical, not moral. The results do not prove intent or corruption. Publishing per-referee analyses is comparable to analysing the performance of players or managers — an accepted practice in sports journalism, provided it is accompanied by adequate methodological contextualisation.
8. Artificial Intelligence Usage Statement

items: Tools used: Claude (Anthropic, 2025–2026), as a programming and documentation assistant.
Scope: generation of Python, SQL and HTML code; technical documentation; structuring of this paper.

Responsibility: the methodological design, all statistical corrections and validation of results are the sole responsibility of the author. The variance formula correction (v0.8.0) and the global FDR correction (v0.9.1) were identified through independent critical review and validated by the author prior to implementation.
Data: no data was generated or modified by AI. All sources are public and verifiable.

content.text: 9. Conclusion

The CAL system represents a contribution to the quantitative analysis of refereeing in the Portuguese Primeira Liga. Following three rounds of critical review, version v0.9.1 corrects two identified methodological errors: the binomial variance formula and the application of FDR as a single global family. The formal overdispersion test confirms the adequacy of the Poisson model with negligible magnitude.

The empirical results are more conservative than the raw Z-scores suggested: 11 foul deviation patterns across 8 distinct referees, none for yellow cards. Three referees with reproducible patterns across two distinct seasons constitute the strongest evidence — inter-season reproducibility substantially reduces the probability of a chance result.

The system operates at the level of a solid exploratory analytical tool. To reach the level of strong scientific inference instrument, the following would be required: minute-level event data; mixed-effects models; and control for team playing styles.
References

items: Agresti, A. (2002). Categorical Data Analysis (2nd ed.). Wiley.
Benjamini, Y., & Hochberg, Y. (1995). Controlling the false discovery rate: a practical and powerful approach to multiple testing. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B, 57(1), 289–300.
Cameron, A. C., & Trivedi, P. K. (1990). Regression-based tests for overdispersion in the Poisson model. Journal of Econometrics, 46(3), 347–364.
Dixon, M., & Robinson, M. (1998). A birth process model for association football matches. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series D, 47(3), 523–538.
Dohmen, T. (2008). The influence of social forces: Evidence from the behavior of football referees. Economic Inquiry, 46(3), 411–424.
Garicano, L., Palacios-Huerta, I., & Prendergast, C. (2005). Favoritism under social pressure. Review of Economics and Statistics, 87(2), 208–216.
Gelman, A., & Hill, J. (2007). Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models. Cambridge University Press.
James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2013). An Introduction to Statistical Learning. Springer.
Karlis, D., & Ntzoufras, I. (2003). Analysis of sports data by using bivariate Poisson models. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series D, 52(3), 381–393.
Lewis, M. (2003). Moneyball: The Art of Winning an Unfair Game. W. W. Norton & Company.
Nevill, A. M., Balmer, N. J., & Williams, A. M. (2002). The influence of crowd noise and experience upon refereeing decisions in association football. Psychology of Sport and Exercise, 3(4), 261–272.
Scoppa, V. (2021). Social pressure in the stadiums: Do agents change behavior without crowd support? Journal of Economic Psychology, 82, 102344.
Sutter, M., & Kocher, M. G. (2004). Favoritism of agents: The case of referees' home bias. Journal of Economic Psychology, 25(4), 461–469.
European Union (2024). Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council on Artificial Intelligence (EU AI Act).

articles.title: Open-Source GIS Architecture for Municipal Land Management
articles.subtitle: A full-stack technical proposal for integrating PostGIS, GeoServer and Apache Airflow in a Portuguese municipality
articles.excerpt: A detailed architectural description of a phased open-source GIS implementation designed for a municipality of 120,000 inhabitants. Covers automated Sentinel-2 pipeline for forest fire risk monitoring, agricultural cadastre integration, municipal infrastructure inventory and a public transparency portal — with technology selection rationale, data migration strategy and cost breakdown across three scope options.

articles.title: EconoStats Portugal: From Zero to Production in Two Weeks
articles.subtitle: How combining economic indicators with a GIS layer creates something that doesn't yet exist in Portugal — and why it matters
articles.excerpt: EconoStats Portugal went from an empty repository to a live Django application with 4,015 economic indicators, a PostGIS-backed GIS layer for industrial clusters, five automated data pipelines, and an AI chatbot — in under two weeks of active development. This article describes what was built, the technical decisions behind it, and why the combination of macroeconomic data with geographic context is rarer and more valuable than it first appears.

content.text: Pedro M. Pestana · TerraDigital · Torres Vedras, Portugal · May 2026
1. The Problem Space

Portugal has good public economic data. The Ministry of Economy publishes the GEE bulletin monthly — a comprehensive Excel workbook covering industrial activity, prices, trade, tourism, and business sentiment. INE publishes GDP, employment, and price series through a REST API. Banco de Portugal publishes the leading coincident indicator daily. Eurostat makes all of this comparable across EU member states. The data is there, it is public, and it is reasonably well-structured.

What doesn't exist is a single place that assembles this data, contextualises it geographically, and makes it readable for someone who isn't an economist. There is no Portuguese site that shows you, on the same screen, that the paper and pulp sector grew 3.2% last year and where The Navigator Company and Celbi have their production facilities. No site that lets you look at regional unemployment rates on a map and simultaneously understand how they relate to the industrial composition of each region.
That gap is what EconoStats Portugal was built to fill.
2. What Was Built

EconoStats Portugal went online on 9 May 2026. By the end of Phase 2 (24 May 2026), the system had 14 pages in production, 4,015 economic indicators in the database, 33 geocoded companies, five automated data pipelines, a D3.js choropleth for Portugal's NUTS3 regions, and an AI chatbot with page-level context. The full stack:

items: Django 6.0.5 + GeoDjango — application framework and spatial ORM
PostgreSQL 16 + PostGIS 3.4 — spatial database with GIST indexes on company locations
Gunicorn + Nginx — production deployment on Hetzner VPS (88.198.95.103)
Leaflet.js — interactive company location maps with sector filters
D3.js + TopoJSON — NUTS3 choropleth using Eurostat 2021 boundaries
Chart.js 4.4.1 — time series, bar charts, doughnut charts throughout
Alpine.js — lightweight interactivity for tooltips and filters
HTMX — partial page updates without a JavaScript framework
API Anthropic (claude-sonnet-4) — contextual chatbot with verified structural knowledge

content.text: The database schema is minimal but purposeful: five tables handle everything. Subsetor stores the 12 industrial subsectors with their CAE codes and sector weights. Indicador stores 4,015 time-series data points with source, type, period, and value. Empresa holds company fundamentals. UnidadeLocal links companies to geographic coordinates via a PostGIS Point geometry in WGS84. PipelineLog records every pipeline execution — source, status, record counts, duration in seconds — visible in the admin panel and in the footer of every page.
3. The Data Pipeline

Five cron-scheduled Django management commands handle all data ingestion. The primary one — fetch_gee — runs daily at 08:00 and is worth describing in detail because it illustrates a real production challenge: the GEE bulletin URL changes with every publication.

The naive approach would be to hardcode the Excel URL and update it manually when it breaks. Instead, the pipeline starts by scraping the GEE website to find the current href pointing to a .xlsx file, then downloads it. This single decision eliminated an entire category of maintenance failures. The Excel workbook has 25 sheets; the parser handles two distinct structures — horizontal layout for monthly conjunctural series (industrial activity index, HICP, industrial production, business sentiment) and vertical layout for annual national accounts data.

Every insert uses Django ORM's update_or_create with a composite key of (name, source, type, period, date). The pipeline is fully idempotent — running it multiple times on the same data produces exactly the same database state. Last measured execution: 1,653 records processed in 8.2 seconds.

The four remaining pipelines cover Eurostat data. Two run daily (conjunctural: unemployment, HICP, quarterly GDP by country), two run weekly on Sundays (structural: public debt, deficit, GDP per capita in PPS, labour market indicators by country, regional unemployment by NUTS2). All log to PipelineLog; a failure in any pipeline leaves the site unaffected — it simply shows slightly older data until the next successful run.
4. The GIS Layer
This is where EconoStats differs from every other Portuguese economic data site, and it is worth explaining the design choices carefully.

The first question was what to put on the map. GDP aggregates and employment statistics are regional data — they belong on a choropleth. Company locations are point data — they belong on a Leaflet map with popups. Trying to put both on the same map creates visual noise without analytical clarity. The decision was to build two distinct GIS components: a Leaflet map for company locations (already in production), and a D3.js choropleth using TopoJSON for NUTS3 regional data (completed in Phase 2).

The Leaflet map currently shows 33 geocoded companies across 8 industrial subsectors. Each point is sized proportionally to estimated employee count. Points pulse if the company's subsector had positive growth in 2025; they are static if growth was negative. Clicking a point opens a full company card: legal name, NIPC, primary CAE code, municipality, estimated headcount, share capital, economic group, and year-on-year variation. The map connects bidirectionally to sector pages — each sector detail page has a 'View on GIS Map' button that opens the map pre-filtered to that sector, using a URL hash parameter.

The choropleth covers all 25 Portuguese NUTS3 regions using the Eurostat 2021 administrative boundaries. The reverse geocoding — assigning each company to its NUTS2 and NUTS3 region — was done entirely in PostGIS using a spatial join rather than an API call. This is faster, reproducible, and works offline.

-- NUTS3 assignment via PostGIS spatial join
UPDATE core_unidadelocal ul
SET nuts3 = n.nuts_id
FROM nuts3_boundaries n
WHERE ST_Within(ul.geom, n.geom);
NUTS3 assignment using PostGIS ST_Within — no external API required
5. Why This Combination Is Rare

The question worth asking is: why doesn't this already exist? The data is public. The tools are free. Django and PostGIS are mature and well-documented. The answer reveals something about how analytical projects tend to be scoped.

Economic data portals are typically built by economists or by public statistics agencies. Their mental model of the data is tabular: series, periods, values. A choropleth is, at most, an illustration — something added after the analytical work is done, to make a report look better. The geographic layer is an afterthought because geography is not where the economic analysis happens in that tradition.

GIS platforms, on the other hand, are typically built around land use, environmental monitoring, infrastructure, or cadastre — domains where the spatial dimension is primary and economic data appears only as an attribute. The GIS practitioner thinks in layers and geometries; a time series of monthly industrial activity indices is not a natural object in that world.

EconoStats sits at the intersection of these two traditions by design. The economic data is not decoration on a map, and the map is not decoration on a statistics dashboard. They are two views of the same underlying question: what is happening in the Portuguese productive economy, and where is it happening?

This intersection matters most for three types of analysis that become straightforward in EconoStats but are difficult or impossible with either a pure statistics portal or a pure GIS platform: understanding regional economic divergence (why is unemployment in the Algarve structurally different from unemployment in the Norte, given their different industrial compositions?), identifying geographic exposure to sector shocks (which municipalities depend heavily on subsectors showing negative indicators?), and contextualising company performance within its regional economic environment.
6. What Is Still Missing
Two weeks of active development gets you to an MVP, not a finished product. The honest accounting of what Phase 3 and beyond need to address:

items: Company data scale — 33 geocoded companies across 8 subsectors is a proof of concept. Meaningful sectoral analysis requires 300–500 companies. The limitation is not technical; it is data sourcing. There is no free, structured, programmatic source of Portuguese company data with addresses. The current strategy involves AICEP export lists, sector association member directories (ATP for textiles, CELPA for pulp and paper, ACAP for automotive), and manual curation from public commercial registries.

INE and Banco de Portugal pipelines — the GEE bulletin is the backbone, but it does not cover everything. The INE REST API and BPstat API are mapped but not yet implemented. Adding them expands coverage to quarterly GDP components, detailed employment data, and the coincident indicator with its full historical series.
English version — all 14 pages are in Portuguese. An /en/ path is planned for Phase 4.

Sector-level narrative context — the data is there, but interpreting it requires knowing that a 2% drop in the industrial activity index in textiles is different from a 2% drop in automotive, because the structural dynamics, export exposure, and employment concentration are different. Adding curated interpretive content to each sector page is editorial work, not engineering.

content.text: 7. The Technical Decisions That Held Up
Two weeks of intensive development always produces a list of things to fix. But some decisions proved robust from the start and are worth documenting as such.

Django management commands for data pipelines, rather than standalone scripts or a workflow orchestrator, was the right choice at this scale. The commands run in the Django ORM context, have access to all models and settings, are callable from cron without any additional infrastructure, and integrate naturally with the admin panel via PipelineLog. The operational overhead is minimal.

PostGIS for all spatial operations, rather than doing geometry calculations in Python, eliminated an entire class of problems. Spatial joins, point-in-polygon queries for NUTS assignment, distance calculations — all handled in SQL, at the database layer, with proper indexing. The codebase has no Shapely or Fiona imports.

Alpine.js for frontend interactivity rather than React or Vue kept the template layer simple. The sector filter on the company map, tooltip display, and form interactions are all handled with x-data attributes directly in the HTML templates. No build step, no node_modules, no compilation.

Idempotent upsert operations in every pipeline means that re-running any pipeline after a failure, a data correction, or a schema migration produces a consistent database state. This is a discipline requirement, not a feature — but getting it right from the start saves significant debugging time later.
8. Artificial Intelligence Usage Statement

items: Tools used: Claude (Anthropic, 2025–2026), as a programming and documentation assistant.
Scope: generation of Python, SQL, HTML, and JavaScript code; technical documentation; structuring of this article.
Responsibility: system architecture, all technical decisions, and validation of outputs are the sole responsibility of the author.
Data: no data was generated or modified by AI. All sources are public and verifiable (GEE, Eurostat, INE, Banco de Portugal).

EU AI Act classification: minimal risk system. EconoStats presents public institutional data with source attribution. The AI chatbot operates with verified structural knowledge and explicit uncertainty acknowledgement.
Testing and results: all functional testing, pipeline validation, and verification of results were carried out by the author.

content.text: 9. Conclusion

EconoStats Portugal is not a finished product. It is a working foundation that demonstrates something specific: that the combination of macroeconomic time-series data with a geographically-aware application layer is both technically straightforward and analytically valuable — and that this combination is rare in the Portuguese information landscape not because it is difficult, but because the two traditions that produce these tools rarely overlap.
The site is live at econostats.terradigital.net. The architecture document is available in the TerraDigital portfolio. Phase 3 is in progress.

articles.title: NaturalMed Wellness: Building a Static Site for a Domain I Didn't Know
articles.subtitle: How understanding Traditional Chinese Medicine shaped every technical decision in a zero-cost, fully-featured bilingual website
articles.excerpt: Building a website for a Traditional Chinese Medicine clinic in Wales meant learning enough about TCM to ask the right questions — and then making every technical decision serve a practitioner who needed to publish articles, manage newsletters, and comply with both UK and EU GDPR, with zero ongoing hosting cost.

content.text: Pedro M. Pestana · TerraDigital · Torres Vedras, Portugal · May 2026
1. The Brief

The client — a Traditional Chinese Medicine practitioner running a clinic in Wales, UK — needed a bilingual website (English and Portuguese) that could be updated without technical knowledge, published articles in both languages, sent newsletters to patients, and complied with data protection regulations on both sides of the post-Brexit divide: UK GDPR and EU GDPR simultaneously.

The constraint that shaped everything else: zero ongoing hosting cost. The practitioner runs a small independent clinic and could not commit to monthly infrastructure costs. Every technical decision flowed from that constraint.
2. The Domain Problem

Before writing a single line of code, I spent considerable time understanding Traditional Chinese Medicine well enough to ask useful questions. TCM has a specific vocabulary — Qi, meridians, tongue diagnosis, pulse diagnosis, the Five Elements, Zang-Fu organ theory — and a specific patient journey that differs substantially from Western medicine. A patient doesn't book an appointment for a 'problem'; they come for a 'condition' that is understood through a diagnostic framework that integrates physical symptoms, emotional state, sleep patterns, and dietary habits.

This mattered for the site architecture because the content structure had to reflect how a TCM practitioner thinks about their work, not how a generic health clinic presents its services. The 'What I Treat' section is not a list of conditions but a set of patterns. The 'About' section is not a CV but an explanation of a diagnostic philosophy. Getting this right required multiple rounds of structured questioning with the practitioner — treating the requirements gathering as a domain investigation.
3. The Stack

The zero-cost constraint pointed directly to GitHub Pages for hosting. The practitioner's requirement to update content without technical knowledge pointed to a headless CMS. The combination of these two constraints — static hosting with Git-based content management — led to Sveltia CMS, authenticated via a Cloudflare Workers OAuth proxy that mediates between the browser-based CMS and the GitHub API without exposing credentials.

items: GitHub Pages — static hosting, zero cost, automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt
Sveltia CMS — browser-based Git CMS, edits files directly in the GitHub repository
Cloudflare Workers — OAuth proxy for GitHub authentication (free tier)
GitHub Actions — build pipeline triggered on every commit
build_articles.py — Python script that converts Markdown articles to HTML, updates the article listing, and generates the newsletter
Vanilla HTML/CSS/JavaScript — no framework, no node_modules, no build step for the site itself

content.text: 4. The Build Pipeline

The most technically interesting component is the build pipeline. When the practitioner writes a new article in Sveltia CMS and saves it, a commit is made to the repository. GitHub Actions detects the commit and runs build_articles.py, which does three things in sequence: converts the Markdown file to a styled HTML article page, updates the articles listing page with the new entry, and generates an HTML newsletter version of the article formatted for email clients.

The newsletter generation was the most constrained part of the build. Email clients — particularly Outlook — have notoriously limited CSS support. The newsletter HTML uses table-based layout with inline styles, no external stylesheets, no JavaScript, and images referenced by absolute URL. The output is a single self-contained HTML file that can be sent via Gmail's BCC-based newsletter system without a paid email platform.

The pipeline runs in under 30 seconds. The practitioner's workflow is: open the CMS at /admin/, write an article, click Save. Two minutes later the article is live on the site and a newsletter-ready HTML file is available for download.
5. GDPR: Two Jurisdictions Simultaneously

Post-Brexit UK GDPR and EU GDPR are substantively similar but technically distinct legal frameworks. The clinic is based in Wales and serves patients in the UK; the website is accessible from the EU; the developer is based in Portugal. This created a genuine dual-jurisdiction compliance requirement.

The cookie consent implementation had to satisfy both frameworks without being unnecessarily complex. The solution: a single consent banner with three categories (necessary, analytics, marketing), stored preferences in localStorage, conditional loading of any analytics scripts, and a privacy policy that explicitly addresses both UK ICO and EU supervisory authority requirements. No analytics are loaded until explicit consent is given — the site runs fully without any tracking for visitors who decline.

One specific challenge: Cloudflare's email obfuscation, which was active on the domain and was scrambling the contact email addresses in the HTML before they reached the browser. This required disabling Cloudflare's automatic email obfuscation for the domain while maintaining the rest of Cloudflare's CDN benefits — a non-obvious configuration step that took longer to diagnose than to fix.
6. What Made This Project Different

Most web development projects begin with a brief that is already translated into technical requirements: 'we need a blog, a contact form, and a booking widget.' This project required working backwards from a domain — TCM — to derive what the technical requirements should be. The practitioner knew what they needed to communicate; they didn't know how to express that as a website structure.

The most valuable skill deployed in this project was not any particular technology but the ability to ask domain questions systematically: What does a new patient need to understand before booking? What do existing patients look for when they return to the site? What is the difference between a condition and a pattern in TCM terms, and how does that affect the navigation structure? The technical implementation followed naturally once these questions had answers.
7. Results

items: Zero ongoing hosting cost — GitHub Pages free tier handles all traffic
Bilingual EN/PT — all 8 pages and articles available in both languages
CMS-managed content — practitioner publishes articles without technical assistance
Automated newsletter — HTML email generated automatically from each article
GDPR compliant — UK GDPR + EU GDPR cookie consent implemented
iOS Safari compatible — hamburger menu debugging resolved a specific WebKit rendering issue
Appointment booking managed via the NaturalMed Clinic Manager — the Google Calendar embed was removed and replaced by the clinic's dedicated management system

content.text: 8. Artificial Intelligence Usage Statement

items: Tools used: Claude (Anthropic, 2025–2026), as a programming and documentation assistant.
Scope: generation of HTML, CSS, JavaScript and Python code; technical documentation; structuring of this article.
Responsibility: system architecture, all technical decisions, requirements gathering and domain research are the sole responsibility of the author.
Data: no patient data was processed or stored in the development of this project.
Testing and results: all functional testing, user acceptance testing with the practitioner, and validation of results were carried out by the author.

content.text: 9. Conclusion

The NaturalMed Wellness website demonstrates that a zero-cost, fully-featured bilingual site with CMS-managed content, automated build pipelines, newsletter generation, and dual-jurisdiction GDPR compliance is achievable with GitHub Pages, Cloudflare Workers, and a disciplined vanilla HTML stack. The most important investment was not in the technology but in understanding the domain well enough to make the right structural decisions before writing any code.
The site is live at naturalmed-wellness.com.

articles.title: NaturalMed Clinic Manager: Building a Clinical System for a Domain You've Never Worked In
articles.subtitle: How iterative development and domain investigation produced a full-featured TCM practice management system over three months of solo development
articles.excerpt: Building a clinic management system for a Traditional Chinese Medicine practitioner meant learning a clinical domain from scratch — SOAP notes, tongue and pulse diagnosis, herbal formula libraries, the Stems and Branches calendar system — and translating that knowledge into a Django application with 19 models across 9 apps, deployed on Hetzner with Docker Compose, multi-factor authentication, and automated daily backups.

content.text: Pedro M. Pestana · TerraDigital · Torres Vedras, Portugal · May 2026
1. The Starting Point

The brief was clear in its outcome but opaque in its domain: build a clinic management system for a Traditional Chinese Medicine practitioner. The outcome — something to manage patients, appointments, treatments, prescriptions, and billing — maps onto familiar application patterns. The domain — TCM clinical practice — does not.

TCM is not a simplified version of Western medicine. It has its own diagnostic framework, its own clinical vocabulary, its own theory of the body, and its own treatment modalities. A TCM practitioner does not diagnose a 'condition' in the ICD-10 sense; they identify a 'pattern' — a combination of signs and symptoms interpreted through the lens of Qi, Blood, Yin/Yang balance, and the Zang-Fu organ system. The clinical records that serve Western medicine are structurally inadequate for TCM practice.

This meant that before designing a single database model, I had to understand enough TCM to ask the right questions. What does a new patient intake look like? What gets recorded at each treatment session? How are herbal prescriptions structured? What is the Stems and Branches system and why does it matter clinically? The answers shaped every architectural decision.
2. What Was Built

The NaturalMed Clinic Manager is a Django 5.1 application with 19 database models across 9 apps, deployed on Hetzner Cloud with Docker Compose. Development ran over approximately three months as a solo project, with the scope expanding iteratively as clinical testing revealed new requirements.

items: tenants — multi-tenancy for multiple clinic instances under a single deployment
accounts — user management with role-based access and two-factor authentication (django-two-factor-auth)
patients — patient intake, demographic data, clinical history, consent management
appointments — FullCalendar.js integration, appointment types, status tracking
treatment — SOAP notes, TCM tongue and pulse diagnosis with photography upload, treatment history
billing — invoicing with VAT, PDF export via WeasyPrint, service catalogue with Quick Select
stock — inventory management, purchase orders, supplier management
expenses — expense tracking with category breakdown and financial summary charts
prescriptions — herbal formula library, custom formula builder, dosage and preparation notes

content.caption: Figure 1 — The operational dashboard: today's scheduled and attended appointments, new patients, revenue, monthly treatments, active patients, cancellation rate, average revenue per appointment, financial summary, and stock alerts — all on a single screen.

images.caption: Figure 2 — Weekly appointment calendar with colour-coded status: Scheduled, Confirmed, Attended, Cancelled, No-show. Month/Week/Day views.

images.alt: NaturalMed Clinic Manager — Patient record with Ganzhi calculator
images.caption: Figure 3 — Patient record showing the Stems and Branches (干支 Ganzhi) calculator: birth date converted to Year, Month, and Day Pillars with elemental and organ system correspondences.

content.text: 3. The TCM Clinical Layer
The most distinctive technical component is the clinical layer — the set of features that distinguish this from a generic appointment and billing system. Three deserve specific mention.
SOAP Notes with TCM Extensions

Standard SOAP notes (Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan) are the baseline of clinical documentation. The TCM extension adds a structured diagnosis section that captures tongue characteristics (colour, coating, shape, moisture), pulse qualities at the three positions on each wrist (cun, guan, chi — each mapped to specific organ systems), and the resulting pattern diagnosis in TCM terms. Tongue photographs can be uploaded directly from the treatment session and stored against the clinical record.
Herbal Formula Library

Chinese herbal medicine uses classical formulas — combinations of herbs with established proportions and indications — as the starting point for individualised prescriptions. The formula library stores both classical formulas and the practitioner's custom modifications, with each herb recorded by its Chinese name (pinyin), Latin botanical name, typical dosage range, and clinical actions. A prescription is built by selecting a base formula and adjusting individual herb quantities for the specific patient presentation.
Stems and Branches (Ganzhi 干支) Calculator

The Stems and Branches system is a traditional Chinese calendrical and cosmological framework that maps time — years, months, days, hours — onto a cycle of ten Heavenly Stems and twelve Earthly Branches. In TCM, a patient's birth date expressed in Ganzhi terms carries clinical significance for understanding constitutional tendencies. The calculator converts any Gregorian date to its Ganzhi equivalent and displays the associated elemental and organ system correspondences. This was the most intellectually unfamiliar component to implement — it required understanding a calendrical system with no direct Western equivalent.
4. Iterative Development Under Domain Uncertainty

The most significant methodological challenge was building a system for a domain I was learning as I built it. The standard approach — gather all requirements, design the full schema, then implement — was not viable because neither I nor the practitioner could fully specify the requirements in advance. TCM clinical practice has subtleties that only emerge through use.

The solution was genuine iterative development: build the minimum viable version of each feature, put it in front of the practitioner, and let clinical testing reveal what was missing or wrong. The prescription module went through four significant revisions as the practitioner's actual workflow — which differed from what either of us had anticipated — became clear through use. The billing Quick Select feature emerged entirely from observing that entering service line items one by one was too slow for the end-of-session workflow.

This approach has a cost: the schema accumulated some structural debt as requirements evolved. The treatment model, for example, has a more complex relationship to the appointment model than a greenfield design would produce. The trade-off was deliberate — a working system with manageable debt is preferable to a theoretically clean system that doesn't match the practitioner's actual workflow.
5. Infrastructure
The deployment stack runs on a Hetzner Cloud VPS with Docker Compose. The composition:

items: Django 5.1 + Gunicorn — application server
PostgreSQL 16 — primary database
Redis — session storage and Celery message broker
Celery — asynchronous task queue for PDF generation and email
Nginx — reverse proxy and static file serving
Let's Encrypt + Certbot — automatic SSL certificate provisioning and renewal
Automated daily backups — PostgreSQL dump to remote storage, 30-day retention

content.text: Multi-tenancy is implemented at the application level via a tenant identifier on each model. This allows multiple clinic instances — the practitioner may open a second location — to share a single deployment with complete data isolation. Two-factor authentication is mandatory for all accounts via django-two-factor-auth with TOTP.
6. What This Project Demonstrates

The NaturalMed Clinic Manager demonstrates something specific about the kind of software development that TerraDigital does: the willingness to enter an unfamiliar domain, invest in understanding it properly, and build systems that reflect the actual structure of the domain rather than forcing the domain into a generic template.

A generic clinic management system would have served some of the practitioner's needs. It would not have captured tongue and pulse diagnosis, would not have understood the structure of Chinese herbal formulas, and would not have included the Ganzhi calculator. These are not decorative features — they are the clinical record that a TCM practitioner actually needs. Building them required domain investigation, not just programming.
7. Artificial Intelligence Usage Statement

items: Tools used: Claude (Anthropic, 2025–2026), as a programming and documentation assistant.
Scope: generation of Python, SQL, HTML and JavaScript code; technical documentation; structuring of this article.
Responsibility: system architecture, all technical decisions, domain research, and clinical requirements gathering are the sole responsibility of the author.
Data: the system handles clinical patient data. All data protection measures — encryption in transit, access controls, audit logging — were designed and implemented by the author.
Testing and results: all functional testing, clinical workflow validation with the practitioner, and verification of results were carried out by the author.

content.text: 8. Conclusion

Three months of solo development produced a clinical system that a TCM practitioner uses daily to manage their practice. The system covers the full operational scope — patient management, appointments, clinical records, prescriptions, billing, stock, and expenses — with clinical features specific to TCM practice that no off-the-shelf system provides.

The most important lesson from this project is that domain knowledge is not optional when building specialist software. The time invested in understanding TCM before writing code was not overhead — it was the design work that made everything else possible.
